1: Hear now what the LORD says: Arise, plead your case before the mountains — shim'u-na et-asher YHWH omer: the third major lawsuit (rib) opening in the book. Qum riv et-he-harim (arise, plead your case before the mountains) — YHWH summons Israel to a cosmic courtroom where the mountains serve as ancient witnesses (cf. Deuteronomy 32:1; Isaiah 1:2). The mountains are addressed as yesode arets (enduring foundations of the earth) — witnesses from creation's beginning.
2: Hear, O mountains, the LORD's indictment — shim'u harim et-riv YHWH: the mountains are summoned to hear and validate YHWH's legal case. Ve-ha-etanim mosdei arets (you enduring foundations of the earth) — etanim (perennial, enduring — same root as the ever-flowing stream in Amos 5:24); the most permanent features of creation are the witnesses. Ki-riv le-YHWH im-ammo (for the LORD has an indictment against his people) — the rib (legal controversy, lawsuit) pattern established in OT prophetic rhetoric.
3: O my people, what have I done to you? In what have I wearied you? Testify against me! — ammi meh-'asiti lakh u-mah hel'etikha aneih-vi: YHWH turns the lawsuit into a direct appeal — almost a reverse lawsuit: testify against me if you have a complaint. Hel'etikha (I have wearied you) — la'ah (to tire, weary, be burdened); YHWH challenges Israel to name any way in which covenant faithfulness to him was an unreasonable burden. This is perhaps the most intimate and vulnerable divine speech in the OT prophets.
4: For I brought you up from the land of Egypt and redeemed you from the house of slavery — ki he'elitikha me-erets Mitsrayim: the exodus recital as YHWH's defense — evidence of faithfulness. Ve-et Mosheh Aharon u-Miryam shalachthi lefanekha (and I sent before you Moses, Aaron, and Miriam) — the only place in the OT where Miriam is listed alongside Moses and Aaron as a leader of the exodus (cf. Exodus 15:20-21). YHWH's defense includes the full leadership team he provided.
5: Remember what Balak king of Moab devised, and what Balaam the son of Beor answered him — the Balaam episode (Num 22-24): Balak hired Balaam to curse Israel; YHWH turned every curse into blessing. Mi-ha-Shittim 'ad-ha-Gilgal (from Shittim to Gilgal) — the two pilgrimage sites bracketing the Jordan crossing: Shittim (east bank, last camp before crossing) and Gilgal (west bank, first camp after crossing, Joshua 4:19-5:12). The crossing itself is YHWH's saving act. Lema'an da'at tsidqot YHWH (so that you may know the saving acts of the LORD) — tsidqot YHWH (righteous acts/saving acts) — the plural tsidqot covers all YHWH's historical acts of covenant faithfulness.
6: With what shall I come before the LORD, and bow myself before God on high? — bamah aqaddem YHWH: the worshiper's question, escalating through five increasingly extravagant offerings. Ha-eqqaddem be-'olot be-'agalim bene-shanah (shall I come with burnt offerings, with year-old calves?) — the most standard sacrifice begins the escalation. The rhetorical movement is designed to show that the problem is not the inadequacy of the offering-type or quantity.
7: Will the LORD be pleased with thousands of rams, with ten thousand streams of oil? — ha-yirtsah YHWH be-alfei elim: the scale escalates to absurdity — ten thousand (riveve) rivers of oil. Then the climax: ha-etten bekori be-pish'i peri vitni le-chatat nafshi (shall I give my firstborn for my transgression, the fruit of my body for the sin of my life?) — the firstborn child (bekori) as the ultimate sacrifice. The reference may target the Moabite king Mesha's child sacrifice (2 Kings 3:27) or the Canaanite practice of molk offerings, which Israel was explicitly prohibited from doing (Leviticus 18:21; Micah 20:2-5).
8: He has shown you, O man, what is good — higgid lekha adam mah-tov: the answer to the five-step escalation of vv6-7. YHWH has already made clear what he requires — the emphatic higgid (he has declared, made known) points back to Torah and prophetic instruction already given. The three requirements: asot mishpat (to do justice — mishpat = procedural, judicial rightness), ahavat chesed (to love covenant faithfulness — chesed = loyal covenant love/kindness), and hatsnea lekhet im-Elohekha (to walk humbly with your God — tsana' = modest, reserved; lekhet = to walk consistently). These three encompass the totality of covenant ethics: justice in public life, covenant loyalty in relationships, and humble walk with God.
9: The voice of the LORD cries to the city — qol YHWH la'ir yiqra': the divine voice addressing the city directly; wisdom recognizes (tushiyyah yir'e shemekha — it is wisdom to fear your name). Shim'u matteh umi yeda'ah (hear, O tribe! who appointed the rod?) — the rod (matteh) as both the tribe and the instrument of punishment; YHWH appointed it, so hearing it is mandatory.
10: Are there yet treasures of wickedness in the house of the wicked — ha-'od ish bet rasha' otsarot resha': the rhetorical question expects a 'yes' answer — yes, they are still there. Ve-ephah razah zu'amah (and the short measure that is accursed) — ephah razah (lean/small ephah) — an undersized measuring container used to defraud in grain transactions; zu'amah (accursed, detested) — YHWH's emotional response to dishonest weights.
11: Shall I declare innocent the one who uses wicked scales — ha-ezkeh be-moznei resha': ezke (shall I acquit/declare innocent) — the legal verdict term. Mozne resha' (wicked/fraudulent scales) and kis avanei mirmah (a bag of deceitful weights) — the two instruments of commercial fraud: the scale beam and the standard weights (avanim, literally 'stones' used as weights). The legal-mercantile question: YHWH cannot issue a verdict of innocent when the instruments of trade are themselves dishonest.
12: Your wealthy people are full of violence — asher 'ashireha male'u chamas: chamas (violence, wrongdoing) filling the wealthy; the acquisition of wealth through force. Ve-yosheveha dibberu shaqer (your residents speak lies) — deceptive speech as standard commercial practice. U-leshsmonam remmiyah be-fihem (and their tongues are deceitful in their mouths) — remmiyah (deceit, treachery) describes the tongue as a standard tool of fraud.
13: Therefore I am striking you with a grievous blow — ve-gam ani hichleiti hakkotekha: YHWH has begun (hichleiti — begun, started) to strike; the judgment is underway. Hashimem 'al-chatta'otekha (devastating you because of your sins) — shalem (devastate, make desolate) as the consequence of chatta'ot (sins).
14: You shall eat, but not be satisfied, and there shall be hunger within you — atah tochal ve-lo tisba': the first covenant-curse reversal: eating without satisfaction (Leviticus 26:26; Deuteronomy 28:38-40). Ve-yeshchakha be-qirbeka (there shall be hunger within you) — the hunger comes from within despite consumption; a metaphor for insatiable greed that also becomes literal famine. Ve-tasseg ve-lo taflit (you shall put away goods, but not save them) — whatever you save, the sword takes.
15: You shall sow, but not reap; you shall press olives, but not anoint yourselves with oil; you shall tread grapes, but not drink the wine — the three agricultural covenant-curses of Deuteronomy 28:38-40 applied in sequence: grain, olive, vine. The pattern is labor without benefit: sow/not reap, press/not anoint, tread/not drink. These were the basis of Israelite prosperity; their inversion signals comprehensive covenant-curse fulfillment.
16: For the statutes of Omri are kept, and all the practices of the house of Ahab — ve-yishsamer chuqqot Omri: Omri (founder of the Omride dynasty, 876-869 BC) established Samaria and his dynasty's syncretistic religious policies; his son Ahab multiplied them. Kol-ma'aseh beit Achav (all the practices of the house of Ahab) — the Ahab era was the nadir of northern syncretism: Baal cult, Asherah worship, judicial murder (Naboth). Following their counsel (ba-attsotam telekhu) means Judah has adopted the same self-destructive trajectory. Le-sheim shemmah ve-livsheqetah 'ammi tissal (that I may make you a desolation, and your inhabitants a hissing) — shemmah (desolation, horror) and sheqetah (hissing — the sound made by onlookers at a ruined city, 1 Kings 9:8).
